title.zh: 大腦「過度警戒」的密碼：眶額葉伽馬波動與強迫症的瞬間對話
title.en: The Brain's Hidden Rhythm: How Gamma Waves in the Orbitofrontal Cortex Drive Obsessive-Compulsive Behaviors

summary.zh:
你是否曾被莫名的恐懼困住，反覆確認門鎖、洗手數十次？這正是強迫症（OCD）患者的日常寫照。儘管科學家長期認為眶額葉皮層（OFC）與OCD症狀有關，但這片腦區究竟如何驅動症狀起伏，始終是未解之謎。最新研究竟能直接探測大腦神經活動，揭開OFC在強迫症中的關鍵角色。研究團隊透過植入電極的intracranial monitoring技術，精細追蹤患者在症狀誘發任務中的神經訊號。結果發現，當患者症狀加劇時，OFC前內側區域的低伽馬頻率活動會明顯升高——就像大腦的「警報器」突然過度敏感，引發一連串強迫行為。

更驚人的是，當研究人員對腦部深層的腹側基底核施以電刺激時，不僅症狀減輕，OFC的伽馬活動也隨之下降。這就像找到一把能同時關閉「警報器」和「強迫循環」的鑰匙。這項發現不僅解釋了OCD症狀的瞬間動態變化，更為未來精準治療提供了新方向。例如，深腦刺激術（DBS）可以針對性地調整特定腦區活動，讓患者擺脫無止境的思維循環。
summary.en:
Have you ever been trapped in a cycle of irrational thoughts and compulsions you simply cannot stop? For people with obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD), this is a daily reality. While scientists have long suspected the orbitofrontal cortex (OFC) plays a role in OCD symptom dynamics, the precise neural mechanisms driving these fluctuations remained unclear—until now. In a groundbreaking study, researchers used intracranial monitoring to record real-time neural activity in the OFC of patients undergoing symptom provocation tasks. They discovered that low-gamma power in the anteromedial OFC consistently spiked during high symptom states, acting like a neural alarm that triggers compulsive behaviors. This finding offers the first direct evidence linking specific brain oscillations to the moment-to-moment expression of OCD symptoms.

The study went further: when researchers stimulated the ventral basal ganglia—a key region connected to the OFC—OCD symptoms decreased alongside reduced gamma activity in the OFC. This dual effect suggests a causal relationship between OFC gamma oscillations and symptom severity, as if the brain’s 'alarm system' could be tuned down. Such insights could revolutionize personalized treatments, such as deep brain stimulation (DBS), which targets specific neural pathways to disrupt the pathological loops underlying OCD. By translating these findings into clinical practice, future therapies may not only manage symptoms but also address their root causes at the neural level.

keyArticle.title: Human orbitofrontal neural activity is linked to obsessive-compulsive behavioral dynamics
